title: Legitimacy

canonical.invariant: Legitimacy is accepted or justified validity of rule or standing within a governance order.

adjacent.authority: Authority concerns who may direct, not whether rule or standing counts as valid.
adjacent.power: Power concerns operative capacity to produce outcomes, not accepted or justified validity.

shortDefinition: Legitimacy is accepted or justified validity of rule or standing within a governance order.
coreMeaning: Within the governance domain, legitimacy functions by marking a rule, office, or standing as valid rather than merely operative. It concerns what counts as justified or accepted, not who may direct or what can be made to happen.

structuralProfile.function: accepted or justified validity assessment of rule or standing
structuralProfile.object: rule, office, or standing within a governance order
structuralProfile.sourceType: acceptance, justification, or procedurally grounded validity
structuralProfile.actorRelation: actor, office, or rule is evaluated as valid rather than merely operative
structuralProfile.temporalRole: evaluative status applied to standing or rule
structuralProfile.enforcementRole: can justify compliance without being enforcement itself
structuralProfile.outcomeAttributionRole: does not attribute outcomes to an actor

fullDefinition:
Legitimacy is not the same as authority or power. In ChatPDM v1 it is defined within the governance domain as accepted or justified validity of rule or standing within a governance order.

Its internal composition joins acceptance or justification to a rule, office, institution, or governing order. The concept therefore evaluates standing rather than conferring directive right or operative capacity.

Within governance structures, legitimacy stabilizes obedience, trust, and continuity by making authority or power count as valid. Power may operate without it, and authority may exist while its legitimacy is contested.

standard.shortDefinition: Legitimacy is accepted or justified validity of rule or standing within a governance order.
standard.coreMeaning: Within the governance domain, legitimacy functions by marking a rule, office, or standing as valid rather than merely operative. It concerns what counts as justified or accepted, not who may direct or what can be made to happen.
standard.fullDefinition:
Legitimacy is not the same as authority or power. In ChatPDM v1 it is defined within the governance domain as accepted or justified validity of rule or standing within a governance order.

Its internal composition joins acceptance or justification to a rule, office, institution, or governing order. The concept therefore evaluates standing rather than conferring directive right or operative capacity.

Within governance structures, legitimacy stabilizes obedience, trust, and continuity by making authority or power count as valid. Power may operate without it, and authority may exist while its legitimacy is contested.

simplified.shortDefinition: Legitimacy means a rule or office counts as valid within a governance order.
simplified.coreMeaning: In governance, legitimacy marks a rule, office, or claim as accepted or justified. It is about valid standing, not about who may direct or what force can do.
simplified.fullDefinition:
Legitimacy is not the same as authority or power. It asks whether rule or standing counts as valid in a governance order.

A government, office, or claim can have power or authority without legitimacy. Legitimacy depends on acceptance, justification, or both.

So legitimacy judges validity. Authority marks who may direct. Power marks what can be made to happen.

formal.shortDefinition: Legitimacy is the accepted or justified validity by which rule or standing counts as proper within a governance order.
formal.coreMeaning: Within the governance domain, legitimacy attributes valid standing to a rule, office, institution, or governing claim rather than merely recording that it operates in fact. It therefore concerns justification or acceptance, not directive competence and not effective capacity as such.
formal.fullDefinition: Legitimacy is defined, within the governance domain, as the accepted or justified validity of rule or standing within a governance order. The concept evaluates whether an office, institution, procedure, or claim counts as proper, and it does so without thereby conferring authority or generating power. Power may remain effective in the absence of legitimacy, and authority may persist while the legitimacy of the underlying order is contested.

subject.label: Rule or standing under evaluative review

basis.description: validity grounded in accepted governance procedure and justificatory order

scope.object: rule, office, or standing within a governance order

contexts.label: governance
contexts.explanation: Legitimacy concerns whether rule, office, or governing standing is treated as valid within an order.

contexts.label: institutional
contexts.explanation: Legitimacy concerns whether decisions, offices, or procedures are accepted as properly grounded.

contexts.label: legal
contexts.explanation: Legitimacy concerns whether formally recognized standing or rule counts as justified rather than merely enforced.

sources.label: David Beetham, The Legitimation of Power
sources.usedFor: structural distinction between valid standing and mere operative power

sources.label: Oxford Languages
sources.usedFor: definition boundary for accepted validity and proper standing

axes.A: accepted validity of a rule, office, or claim
acceptance or justification within an order

axes.statement: legitimacy is not power; power may operate without legitimacy
